coredata.dc:title: N, S co-doped graphene quantum dot–decorated Fe3O4 nanostructures: Preparation, characterization and catalytic activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Sajjadi, Saeed
Khataee, Alireza
Darvishi Cheshmeh Soltani, Reza
Hasanzadeh, Aliyeh
coredata.dc:description: The sonocatalytic decolorization of the dye methylene blue was considered in the present study. In this regard, as a novel approach, Fe3O4 nanoparticles synthesized through a coprecipitation method were decorated with nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (NS-GQDs) and used as a sonocatalyst. The use of NS-GQD-decorated/Fe3O4 (NS-GQD/Fe3O4) nanoparticles significantly increased the decolorization efficiency (99.0%) compared with the use of pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles (43.7%). The band gap of the nanostructured NS-GQD/Fe3O4 (1.923 eV) was lower than that of pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles (2.147 eV). An optimal dopant amount of 3 wt% was used in the experiments. The highest sonocatalytic decolorization of 99.0% was attained at an initial pH of 8. As a result, the adverse effect of scavenging compounds on the sonocatalytic decolorization was in the following order: sulfate < carbonate < chloride. In the case of enhancers, the presence of potassium persulfate and hydrogen peroxide resulted in complete decolorization in a reaction time of 60 min. A drop in the decolorization efficiency of only 4% was observed after the fifth consecutive cycle, thereby implying the appropriate reusability potential of the NS-GQD/Fe3O4 sonocatalyst for use in full-scale operation. Gas chromatography–mass spectrometry analysis was used for the identification of intermediate by-products generated during the sonocatalytic decolorization of methylene blue.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasonic irradiation
Magnetite nanostructures
Doping
Graphene quantum dots
Decolorization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization (SEM, HR-TEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, and VSM analyses)
Effect of coexisting compounds (scavengers and enhancers)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Comparison of sonolysis alone with sonocatalysis processes and (b) the UV–visible absorption spectrum of methylene blue (MB) during the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dot–decorated Fe3O4 (NS-GQD/Fe3O4)/ultrasound (US) process. Initial MB concentration 20 mg/L, Fe3O4 concentration 1 g/L, 3 wt% graphene quantum dot–decorated Fe3O4 (GQD/Fe3O4) concentration 1 g/L, 3 wt% nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot–decorated Fe3O4 (N-GQD/Fe3O4) concentration 1 g/L, 3 wt% NS-GQD/Fe3O4 concentration 1 g/L, pH 9, power 200 W/L. DE, decolorization efficiency.
(Ahν)2-hν curves of (a) Fe3O4 nanoparticles and (b) nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dot–decorated Fe3O4 (NS-GQD/Fe3O4) nanoparticles.
(a) Absorption spectrum of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots and (b) photoluminescence (PL) spectra.

Scanning electron microscopy image of (a) Fe3O4 nanoparticles and (b) nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dot–decorated Fe3O4 (NS-GQD/Fe3O4) nanoparticles. (c) Transmission electron microscopy and (d) high-resolution transmission electron microscopy images of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles. (e) X-ray diffraction patterns of Fe3O4 and NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles. (f) Fourier transform infrared spectra of Fe3O4 and NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles. (g) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectra of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles. (h) Hysteresis loops of Fe3O4 and NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles obtained with a vibrating-sample magnetometer.

Effect of (a) amount of dopant (initial methylene blue [MB] concentration 20 mg/L, pH 8, power 200 W/L), (b) sonocatalyst dosage with 3 wt% nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dot–decorated Fe3O4 (NS-GQD/Fe3O4) (initial MB concentration 20 mg/L, pH 8, power 200 W/L), (c) initial MB concentration (3 wt% NS-GQD/Fe3O4 concentration 1 g/L, pH 8, power 200 W/L), and (d) initial pH (initial MB concentration 20 mg/L, 3 wt% NS-GQD/Fe3O4 concentration 1 g/L, power 200 W/L) on the sonocatalytic decolorization. (e) The pHzpc plot for 3 wt% NS-GQD/Fe3O4 (1 g/L). DE, decolorization efficiency; zpc zero-point charge.

Effect of (a) ultrasonic power (initial methylene blue [MB] concentration 20 mg/L, 3 wt% nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dot–decorated Fe3O4 [NS-GQD/Fe3O4] concentration 1 g/L, pH 8), (b) presence of radical scavengers (initial MB concentration 20 mg/L, 3 wt% NS-GQD/Fe3O4 concentration 1 g/L, pH 8, power 200 W/L, initial scavenger concentration 10 mg/L), and (c) enhancers (initial MB concentration 20 mg/L, 3 wt% NS-GQD/Fe3O4 concentration 1 g/L, pH 8, power 200 W/L, initial enhancer concentration 1 mmol/L) on the decolorization of MB in the presence of 3 wt% NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The activity stability and reusability potential of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanocatalyst in five consecutive experiments (initial MB concentration 20 mg/L, 3 wt% NS-GQD/Fe3O4 concentration 1 g/L, pH 8, power 200 W/L). DE, decolorization efficiency.
Comparison of catalytic activity of some published catalysts in the degradation of methylene blue.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: GQD, graphene quantum dot; GR, graphene; NS-GQD/Fe3O4, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dot–decorated Fe3O4; RGO, reduced graphene oxide.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Identified by-products generated during the degradation of methylene blue.

head.ce:title: N, S co-doped graphene quantum dot–decorated Fe3O4 nanostructures: Preparation, characterization and catalytic activity

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The sonocatalytic decolorization of the dye methylene blue was considered in the present study. In this regard, as a novel approach, Fe3O4 nanoparticles synthesized through a coprecipitation method were decorated with nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (NS-GQDs) and used as a sonocatalyst. The use of NS-GQD-decorated/Fe3O4 (NS-GQD/Fe3O4) nanoparticles significantly increased the decolorization efficiency (99.0%) compared with the use of pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles (43.7%). The band gap of the nanostructured NS-GQD/Fe3O4 (1.923 eV) was lower than that of pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles (2.147 eV). An optimal dopant amount of 3 wt% was used in the experiments. The highest sonocatalytic decolorization of 99.0% was attained at an initial pH of 8. As a result, the adverse effect of scavenging compounds on the sonocatalytic decolorization was in the following order: sulfate < carbonate < chloride. In the case of enhancers, the presence of potassium persulfate and hydrogen peroxide resulted in complete decolorization in a reaction time of 60 min. A drop in the decolorization efficiency of only 4% was observed after the fifth consecutive cycle, thereby implying the appropriate reusability potential of the NS-GQD/Fe3O4 sonocatalyst for use in full-scale operation. Gas chromatography–mass spectrometry analysis was used for the identification of intermediate by-products generated during the sonocatalytic decolorization of methylene blue.

ce:section.ce:para: The elimination of organic dyes from aqueous environments has gained much attention because of their health and environmental adverse impacts [1–4]. Because of the low biodegradability of organic dyes, physicochemical treatment technologies such as adsorption [5,6], electrochemical processes [7], and advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) [8] are applied for the decolorization. The use of AOPs leads to the generation of hydroxyl radical (•OH) with high potential to degrade refractory organic dyes in comparison with conventional oxidation processes [9]. Various AOPs, including photocatalysis [10–13], the Fenton reaction [14], and sonocatalysis [15,16], have been considered and used for treatment of dye-polluted wastewaters. The use of ultrasound (US) in AOPs has received significant attention owing to the special property of US to generate •OH in bulk solution. Moreover, the application of US enhances the mass transfer rate, reduces the consumption of chemicals, and results in the generation of low levels of waste residuals [17–20]. Recently, the application of US in the presence of a suitable sonocatalyst named sonocatalysis has gained more attention for the degradation of organic dyes in the aqueous phase [21]. However, the decomposition of refractory organic dyes with use of a pure catalyst needs a longer reaction time and consequently a larger reactor volume to achieve acceptable degradation. This concern can be overcome by the incorporation of some appropriate elements into the sonocatalyst lattice. Doping is an efficient approach to improve the catalytic activity of catalysts and tune their electrical and optical properties [22–24]. In the present study, Fe3O4 nanoparticles are considered as the main sonocatalyst for the decolorization of dye-polluted solutions under ultrasonic irradiation. Before this, magnetite (Fe3O4) nanoparticles had been successfully applied for the sonocatalytic removal of organic dyes [25,26]. It was demonstrated that the use of only US is inefficient for the complete degradation of refractory organic compounds or for acceptable degradation to by-products with lower toxicity [17]. To overcome this drawback, Fe3O4 was considered as an efficient sonocatalyst for enhancement of the generation of •OH and increase of the degradation efficiency [27].

However, in the present study, efforts were made to improve the catalytic properties of Fe3O4 nanoparticles through doping. Among various doping agents, graphene is used to improve the optical and electrical performance of Fe3O4 nanoparticles [28–30]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are graphene sheets with a lateral size of less than 100 nm, leading to high dispersive capability in the aqueous phase for efficient interaction with reactants compared with amorphous carbon substances [31–35]. GQDs not only possess perfect solubility, chemical stability, and low toxicity but also exhibit unique optical properties [36–39]. It has been demonstrated that the decoration of the main catalyst with GQDs prevents the aggregation of fine particles in the aqueous phase and thus increases the effective surface area and number of reactive sites for an efficient catalytic reaction [31]. Meanwhile, the interaction between the catalyst and GQDs enhances the catalytic activity of the catalyst through two possible reaction mechanisms. First, because of the upconversion effect, GQDs can absorb long-wavelength light and then emit short-wavelength UV photons. Next, the conduction band of GQDs permits electron transfer from the excited catalyst surface, resulting in charge separation and increase of the number of charge carriers available for reactions [40]. Tsai and Hsu [41] investigated the levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of GQDs via cyclic voltammetry assessments (+1.15 V and −2.6 V versus the normal hydrogen electron, respectively). They mentioned that the CdSe/GQD heterostructure will develop a type II band alignment that could permit charge separation, so significantly increasing the H2 generation rate. In a few investigations, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped GQDs (NS-GQDs) with high catalytic activity were successfully synthesized and used in a lithium battery. In the case of GQDs, the creation of an n→π* transition involving conjugated C = X bonds (X is N, S) results in a dopant state close to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital level of the GQDs [42,43]. In accordance with the abovementioned statements, in the present study, Fe3O4 nanoparticles were firstly synthesized through a simple coprecipitation technique. Then as-prepared magnetic catalyst was decorated with NS-GQDs by a facile hydrothermal method. After the preparation, the characterization of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and NS-GQD-decorated Fe3O4 (NS-GQD/Fe3O4) nanoparticles was performed by means of scanning electron microscopy (SEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Furthermore, the catalytic activity of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 was examined regarding the catalytic decolorization of the dye methylene blue (MB) under ultrasonic irradiation. The surface, structural, magnetic, and optical properties of the NS-GQD/Fe3O4 catalyst were investigated before systematic experiments were conducted to study the decolorization. The intermediate by-products generated during the decolorization of MB were identified by gas chromatograph–mass spectrometry (GC-MS) analysis. To the best of our knowledge, the sonocatalytic properties of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles have not been studied until now.

MB (C16H18ClN3S, λ max = 660 nm, molar mass 319.85 g/mol) was purchased from Hopkin & Williams Ltd (UK). The ethanol (purity of 97%) used was obtained from Daru Co. (Iran) for the preparation of samples. Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), iron (II) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl2·6H2O), citric acid (C6H8O7, 99% pure), ammonia (NH3), thiourea (CH4N2S, 99% pure), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Aldrich (USA). All chemicals and reagents were used as received (without any purification), and distilled water was used in the experiments.

A simple coprecipitation technique was used for the synthesis of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. For this, firstly, 250 mL degassed deionized water was prepared with N2 gas; then specific amounts of FeCl2·6H2O and FeCl3·6H2O (molar ratio of 2:3) were dissolved in as-prepared degassed water. The solution was magnetically agitated at high mixing speed under Ar gas to prevent the oxidation of as-prepared Fe3O4 to Fe2O3. Afterward, 140 mL undiluted ammonia solution was added to the aforementioned solution under magnetic stirring for 45 min to form Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The as-synthesized nanoparticles were washed with ethanol and deionized water repeatedly. Finally, they were dried in an oven at 80 °C until constant weight was achieved [44].

A facile hydrothermal method was used for the preparation of NS-GQDs. For this, thiourea (3 mmol) and citric acid (1 mmol) were dissolved in 90 mL deionized water; then the solution was slowly stirred to produce a homogeneous solution. The clear solution was placed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and transferred to an oven (180 °C for 6 h). The as-synthesized NS-GQDs were separated from the bulk solution with use of ethanol and centrifugation (5000 rpm for 20 min) [22].

As-synthesized Fe3O4 nanoparticles were decorated with NS-GQDs via a hydrothermal method. For this, 1 g Fe3O4 nanoparticles and specific amounts of NS-GQDs (to obtain different amounts of 1, 3, and 5 wt%) were blended in 60 mL deionized water. The suspension was ultrasonicated for 1 h. Subsequently, the homogeneous mixture was transferred to a 100-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and placed in an oven (180 °C for 6 h). Finally, as-prepared nanostructured NS-GQD/Fe3O4 was obtained, washed several times with deionized water and alcohol, and then dried in an oven until constant weight was achieved. For comparison, nitrogen-doped GQD֪–decorated Fe3O4 (N-GQD/Fe3O4) and GQD-decorated Fe3O4 (GQD/Fe3O4) were prepared by the same method with urea and sodium hydroxide, respectively, instead of thiourea [45].

The sonocatalytic activity of as-synthesized NS-GQD/Fe3O4 was examined by the destructive decolorization of MB in bulk solution. An ultrasonic bath (Ultra 8060, James Products, UK) was used to irradiate the solution containing MB at a fixed frequency of 36 kHz. The reactor was placed in the dark (30 min) to determine the role of the adsorption process in the removal of MB. The residual concentration of MB was measured spectrophotometrically (SU 6100 UV–visible spectrophotometer, Philler Scientific, USA) at the maximum-absorption wavelength of 660 nm. The decolorization efficiency (DE) of MB was calculated by means of the following equation:
(1)
DE
( % )
=
[
1 −
(
C
t
C
0
)
]
× 100 ,
where c o is the initial concentration of MB (mg/L) and c
t is the concentration of MB at a specified reaction time t. The decolorization by-products of MB were identified with use of a GC-MS system consisting of an Agilent 6890 gas chromatograph with an HP-5ms capillary column (30 m, 0.25-mm inner diameter) and an Agilent 5973 mass spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, USA).

SEM (MIRA3 field emission gun scanning electron microscope, Tescan, Czech Republic) and HR-TEM (JEM-2200FS, JEOL, Japan) were performed to evaluate the surface morphology of the as-prepared samples. The crystalline structure of the nanoparticles was examined by XRD (X'Pert PRO, PANalytical, Germany). The magnetic properties of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles and pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles were assessed by means of a VSM (Meghnatis Kavir Kashan Co., Kashan, Iran). The photoluminescence spectra of the as-prepared NS-GQDs were recorded with a photoluminescence spectrometer (LS45, PerkinElmer, USA). The functional groups placed on the surface of the catalysts were identified by FT-IR spectroscopy (Tensor 27, Bruker, Germany). XPS assessment of the as-prepared NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles was performed with a photoelectron spectrometer (K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific, UK). The pH drift method was used to estimate the point of zero charge of the nanostructured NS-GQD/Fe3O4.

A comparative study was performed, and the role of each process responsible for the removal of MB was determined. The results provided in Fig. 1 a show that the application of US alone led to a DE of 15.9%. The use of US alone generates •OH in the bulk solution containing water molecules according to the following equation [46]:
(2)
H
2
O
→
U S
O
•
H +
H
•

However, US alone is not efficient to generate enough •OH in the solution for significant decomposition of the target pollutant [47]. The addition of Fe3O4 nanoparticles under ultrasonic irradiation (sonocatalysis) resulted in an enhanced DE of 43.7% in a reaction time of 90 min. The sonocatalysis mechanism in the presence of Fe3O4 nanoparticles can be explained by ‘‘hot spot” and ‘‘sonoluminescence” phenomena [48]. During the ‘hot spot phenomenon, cavitation bubbles are created, grow, and subsequently collapse, producing high pressure (>1800 atm) and high temperature (about 5000 K), resulting in the generation of free oxidizing radicals in the solution as represented by the following equations [49]:
(3)
F
e
3 +
+
H
2
O
→
U S
F
e
2 +
+
O
•
H +
H
+
(4)
O
•
H +
H
•
→
H
2
O
(5)
O
•
H +
O
•
H →
H
2
O
2

In the presence of a solid-phase catalyst such as Fe3O4 nanoparticles, as-generated hydrogen peroxide can produce some oxidizing agents for the enhanced decomposition of the target pollutant [50]:
(6)
H
2
O
2
+
H
•
→
H
2
O +
O
•
H
(7)
2
O
•
H →
H
2
O +
O
•
(8)
H
•
+
O
2
→ H
O
2
•
(9)
H
O
2
•
+ H
O
2
•
→
H
2
O
2
+
O
2

Further, ultrasonic irradiation will result in the creation of wide-wavelength light named the sonoluminescence for the excitation of the sonocatalyst surface to generate •OH in the liquid phase. The sonocatalyst plays a photocatalytic role during the sonoluminescence phenomenon as shown by the following equations [47,51,52]:
(10)
Catalyst
→
U S
h
+
+
e
−
(11)
e
−
+
O
2
→
O
2
• −
(12)
h
+
+
H
2
O →
O
•
H +
H
+

Meanwhile, the sonoluminescence emitted by the collapse of cavitation bubbles may promote the conversion of Fe3+ to Fe2+ in the structure of magnetite nanoparticles, which is beneficial for the efficient decomposition of the target pollutant [49]. In sonocatalysis, the use of a solid catalyst with fine particles provides supplemental nuclei for the creation of cavities, generating more cavitation bubbles in the bulk solution [48]. The application of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 significantly increased the DE (99.0%) because of the decoration of Fe3O4 with NS-GQDs (the decrease of the MB absorption peak illustrated in Fig. 1b). The use of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 resulted in the highest DE in comparison with N-GQD/Fe3O4 (87.4%) and GQD/Fe3O4 (55.3%). A summary of the catalytic activity of some published catalysts in the degradation of MB is presented in Table 1 .

In conclusion, the presence of both nitrogen and sulfur improved the catalytic properties of GQDs to be applied for the decoration of Fe3O4 nanoparticles to improve the catalytic degradation of MB. As can be postulated from Fig. 1a, the enhanced DE of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles was mainly attributed to the presence of nitrogen and/or sulfur, with which GODs were doped, owing to their excellent photoluminescence characteristics [22]. The excited electrons (e–) in the conduction band of the NS-GQDs could be moved into the conduction band of Fe3O4 as a result of sonogenerated heat and light. At the same time, as-generated holes (h+) in the valence band of Fe3O4 nanoparticles could transfer to the valence band of the NS-GQDs. The electrons in the conduction band reacted with hydrogen peroxide (Eq. (9)) to create •OH [53]. To make a reasonable comparison, the band gaps of both pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles and NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles were determined (Fig. 2 ). In this regard, the band gap of both sonocatalysts was calculated via the following equations [54]:
(13)
A h
ν
2
= K
(
h v −
E
g
)
,
(14)
h v =
1240
λ
,
where hν, A, E g, λ, and K are the photon energy (eV), the absorption coefficient, the band gap, the wavelength (nm), and a constant, respectively. The band gap of pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles and NS-GQD/Fe3O4 was determined by our plotting (Ahν)2 versus hν. As a result, the band gap of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 (1.923 eV) was found to be lower than that of pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles (2.147 eV), which led to the higher catalytic activity of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 compared with the pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The lower the band gap, the higher the catalytic activity of the semiconductor [55,56]. The absorption spectrum of NS-GQDs is depicted in Fig. 3 a. It shows a well-defined absorbance peak at 360 nm. The curve obtained indicates that the band edge falls over 360 nm. The synthesized NS-GQDs were excited by light of wavelength 360 nm, which could be attributed to the effect of the size and the presence of surface functional groups of NS-GQDs [36]. The photoluminescence spectra of NS-GQDs were recorded for different excitation wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 3b. This was done to determine the recombination rate of as-generated electron-hole pairs in charge carrier transfer, migration, and trapping. The low photoluminescence intensities indicate a low recombination rate of as-generated electron-hole pairs, which is preferred in the case of the use of catalysts in photo-based reactions such as the sonoluminescence phenomenon [57]. Hence, surface decoration of Fe3O4 nanoparticles with NS-GQDs opened a new path to the efficient use of the photoluminescence phenomenon occurring during sonocatalysis. Sonocatalytic reactions like photocatalytic reactions involve the generation of electron-hole pairs on the surface of the sonocatalyst. The formation of these electron-hole pairs is very important during sonocatalysis to achieve acceptable degradation [58]. The photoluminescence spectra of NS-GQDs exhibited no shift in the emission band when the excitation wavelength was altered in the range from 345 to 425 nm. Similar results were reported by Sudhagar et al. [59] with the use of GQD/TiO2 heterostructures in photoelectrochemical reactors. The sonoluminescence phenomenon caused by US produces light with a wide wavelength range [48]; accordingly, the strong decoration of Fe3O4 nanoparticles with NS-GQDs will lead to enhanced light-harvesting capability of the sonocatalyst regarding the photoluminescence phenomenon, thereby increasing the catalytic activity of the NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles [60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization (SEM, HR-TEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, and VSM analyses)
ce:section.ce:para: SEM was performed to reveal the surface morphology of both pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles and NS-GQD/Fe3O4. The images showed the formation of some structures with particle size less than 100 nm. Fig. 4 a reveals the production of Fe3O4 nanoparticles with distinct shapes during the coprecipitation procedure. The surface morphology of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 can be observed in Fig. 4b. As clearly shown, NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles were spherical with regular forms compared with the pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 4a and b, both Fe3O4 and NS-GQD/Fe3O4 are well dispersed with a nanoscale size distribution with diameters less than 100 nm. TEM was performed to reveal the surface morphology of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles in detail (Fig. 4c). The HR-TEM image (Fig. 4d) shows relatively uniform decoration of NS-GQDs in every part of the surface of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Moreover, no clear aggregation occurred during the synthesis of NS-GQD/Fe3O4.

XRD analysis was performed to reveal the crystallinity of the samples as well as for phase identification (Fig. 4e). For pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the main peaks located 29.9°, 35.2°, 42.8°, 53.5°, 56.9°, 62.5°, 70.6°, and 74.0° were attributed to the (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), (440), (620), and (533) planes of the Fe3O4 (magnetite) crystalline structure with JCPDS card number 89–2355 [61–64]. The high crystallinity of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles showed that the doping process had no significant effect on the crystallinity of the as-synthesized NS-GQD/Fe3O4. Similar results were obtained by Hao et al. [65] in the case of the functionalization of titanium dioxide with GQDs.

FT-IR spectra of pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles and NS-GQD/Fe3O4 are shown in Fig. 4f. In the case of pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the peak centered at 565 cm−1 is associated with the presence of the FeO band of pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The peaks located at around 1450, 1620, and 3430 cm−1 were ascribed to the presence of CO, CO, and OH bands of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, respectively [31,60]. The presence of carboxyl groups on the surface of GQDs has been reported [45]. The intensity of the OH band was altered after the decoration of Fe3O4 nanoparticles with NS-GQDs, which indicated the main role of the OH bond in the synthesis of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Some new peaks, including CN, NCS, and CC bands [22,57], appeared after the decoration of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, and were associated with the presence of NS-GQDs.

As shown in Fig. 4g, the XPS spectrum of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 shows five peaks, at 168.67, 282.36, 396.41, 529.51, and 712.71 eV, which are related to S 2p, C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Fe 2p regions, respectively. These peaks clearly demonstrate that the as-prepared GQDs were doped with nitrogen and sulfur atoms, and the NS-GQDs were successfully attached to the surface of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles.

Magnetic properties of Fe3O4 nanoparticles after their being decorated with NS-GQDs were examined by VSM analysis in comparison with those of the pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Fig. 4h shows appropriate magnetism of Fe3O4 nanoparticles even after decoration with NS-GQDs. The saturation magnetization of pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles and that of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 were 68.8 and 54.1 emu/g, respectively. The lower magnetism of the as-synthesized NS-GQD/Fe3O4 compared with the pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles was ascribed to the antiferromagnetic behavior of NS-GQDs as a dopant. Similar decreased magnetism after decoration of magnetite nanoparticles with other elements has been reported [66,67]. In conclusion, the appropriate magnetic properties of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles make it easy to withdraw them from the reactor with an external magnet, increasing the reusability potential of the sonocatalyst in repeated runs. It is noteworthy that the magnetic particles collected can be rapidly reintroduced into the solution if the magnetic field is turned off [48,68].

The amount of NS-GQDs was changed in the range from 1 to 5 wt% to obtain the optimum amount of dopant. Fig. 5 a shows that increasing the amount of dopant from 1 to 3 wt% resulted in the DE increasing from 59.8% to 99.0%, while an increase to 5 wt% led to an obvious drop in the DE (83.3%). Thus, 3 wt% was optimum for the following experiments. Increasing the amount of NS-GQDs from 1 to 3 wt% resulted in a narrower space charge zone and higher surface barrier, which led to the impressive separation of the electron-hole pairs, while further increase in the amount of NS-GQDs resulted in the space charge layer widening by increasing the depth of light penetration into the Fe3O4 nanoparticle lattice. This accelerated electron-hole recombination and consequently decreased the catalytic activity of the sonocatalyst at an NS-GQD amount of 5 wt%. Meanwhile, the excessive amount of NS-GQDs decorating the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles increases the number of electron-hole recombination sites [45].

To assess the effect of sonocatalyst dosage on the sonocatalytic decolorization of MB, the concentration of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles was varied between 0.25 and 1.0 g/L. As shown in Fig. 5b, increasing the sonocatalyst dosage from 0.25 to 1.0 g/L led to the DE increasing from 75.6% to 99.0%. More •OH was expected to be produced during treatment with higher NS-GQD/Fe3O4 dosages as the main source of •OH was NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanostructures. Therefore, the generation of more •OH resulted in higher DE when enough sonocatalyst was present in the bulk solution of the treatment system. These results can be interpreted by the fact that the number of nuclei increases with increasing amount of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles and the extent of acoustic cavitation also increases along with increasing quantity of the sonocatalyst [27].

The effect of dye concentration on the sonocatalysis over NS-GQD/Fe3O4 was evaluated by our varying the initial concentration from 20 to 40 mg/L. As shown in Fig. 5c, increase of the initial concentration of MB to 40 mg/L resulted in a significant decrease in the DE (60.0%). At the initial concentration of 20 mg/L, nearly complete decolorization of MB occurred in a reaction time of 90 min (99.0%). The DE decreased with increasing MB concentration as the amount of as-generated •OH was constant and not enough to destroy the excessive amounts of dye molecules present in the solution. Moreover, the excessive amounts of dye molecules adsorbed onto the sonocatalyst surface prevent the catalyst nanoparticles from absorbing the energy and heat from the cavitation phenomenon, inhibiting the generation of •OH in the solution. The competition between the intermediate by-products generated during the sonocatalysis over NS-GQD/Fe3O4 and the parent compound might be another potential mechanism for the reduced sonocatalytic decomposition [53].

At initial pH values of 3, 7, 8, 9, 10, and 12, the DE was 55.7%, 74.8%, 99.0%, 99.8%, 91.0%, and 70.3%, respectively (Fig. 5d). The zero-point charge (pHzpc) of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanostructures was determined by the pH drift method; the results are depicted in Fig. 5e. As a result, pHzpc was found to be 7.1, indicating that the surface charge of the sonocatalyst would be positive at pH lower than 7.1. Thus, the decrease of the DE from an initial pH of 9 to an initial pH of 3 could be attributed to the electrostatic repulsion force between the positively charged sonocatalyst and MB as a cationic dye. As shown in Fig. 5d, at an initial pH of 12, the electrostatic attraction between the negatively charged sonocatalyst and MB molecules with a positive charge led to an increasement in the DE in comparison with an initial pH of 3. Overall, the DE decreased in both strong acidic (pH 3) and strong alkaline (pH 12) conditions, which was due to the lower generation of •OH in such conditions compared with an initial pH of 9. The presence of structural Fe(II) in magnetite nanoparticles of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 can enhance the generation of •OH in almost neutral pH conditions [69]. Since the main objective of the present study was to offer an effective and economical oxidizing process, the unchanged pH (8) of the MB solution was used in all the experiments.

The US power plays an important role in degrading the target pollutant in US-based treatment processes. For this reason, the effect of this parameter was investigated at three distinct US powers of 200, 400, and 600 W/L. The results are displayed in Fig. 6 a. Increasing the US power increased the DE. At US input powers of 200, 400, and 600 W/L, the DE was 81.5%, 97.7%, and 100%, respectively, at a reaction time of 45 min. However, no significant increase in the DE was observed as the reaction time gradually increased to 90 min. At the end of 90 min, the DE was 99.0%, 100%, and 100% at US powers of 200, 400, and 600 W/L, respectively. Increasing the US power results in enhancement of the cavitation phenomenon, increasing the formation of cavitation bubbles and generating •OH in the presence of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 in the bulk solution for degradation of the target pollutant. Also, increasing the US power increases ultrasonic waves and their intensity, resulting in an increased mass transfer rate [16]. The enhanced agitation intensity at the NS-GQD/Fe3O4–bulk solution interface and the sequential cleaning of the catalyst surface owing to the increased US power contribute to the increased generation of •OH in the bulk solution for degradation of dye molecules [69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of coexisting compounds (scavengers and enhancers)
ce:section.ce:para:
The effect of scavenging compounds on the sonocatalytic degradation of MB by the NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanostructures was studied to determine the influence of competing compounds on the DE. These experiments were also beneficial to determine the role of as-generated •OH in degrading organic dye molecules. The effect of the presence of sulfate, carbonate, and chloride ions on the DE of MB is depicted in Fig. 6b. In the presence of sulfate, carbonate, and chloride with an initial concentration of 10 mg/L, the DE decreased from 99.0% to 75.7%, 63.5%, and 50.3%, respectively. Accordingly, the adverse effect of scavenging compounds on the DE was in the following order: sulfate < carbonate < chloride. Hence, the presence of chloride ions led to the greatest inhibitory effect on the catalytic performance. The radical-scavenging properties of the aforementioned species are represented in the following equations [8]:
(15)
C
O
3
2 −
+
O
•
H → C
O
3
• −
+ O
H
−
(16)
S
O
4
2 −
+
O
•
H → S
O
4
• −
+ O
H
−
(17)
C
l
−
+
O
•
H → C
l
•
+ O
H
−

In conclusion, the decreased DE in the presence of radical-scavenging compounds confirmed the major role of as-generated •OH in degrading the target pollutant by NS-GQD/Fe3O4-catalyzed ultrasonic irradiation. The effect of the presence of enhancing compounds such as potassium persulfate and hydrogen peroxide on the DE was evaluated; the results are presented in Fig. 6c. As expected, the addition of potassium persulfate and hydrogen peroxide increased the DE. The presence of potassium persulfate and hydrogen peroxide resulted in the complete degradation of MB in a reaction time of 60 min, while the DE of MB was found to be 95.6% in the absence of enhancers at the same reaction time. The enhancing ability of potassium persulfate was greater than that of hydrogen peroxide. Under ultrasonic irradiation, decomposition of hydrogen peroxide molecules, along with their reaction with as-generated hydrogen radicals, could result in the production of •OH in the bulk solution as represented by the following equations [45]:
(18)
H
2
O
2
→
U S
2
O
•
H
(19)
H
2
O
→
U S
O
•
H +
H
•
(20)
H
2
O
2
+
H
•
→
H
2
O +
O
•
H

The leaching of iron ions also intensifies the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide to •OH in the bulk solution [69]:
(21)
F
e
2 +
+
H
2
O
2
→
O
•
H

Thus, the integration of iron ions in the structure of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 and hydrogen peroxide increased the generation of free oxidizing radicals. Further, the addition of persulfate ions also enhanced the production of •OH and consequently increased the DE as shown by the following equations [47]:
(22)
S
2
O
8
2 −
→
U S
2 S
O
4
• −
(23)
S
O
4
• −
+
H
2
O → S
O
4
2 −
+
O
•
H +
H
+

The capability of NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanostructures to be used in repeated operational runs was checked in five consecutive experimental runs. After the completion of each experimental run (90 min), the spent sonocatalyst was withdrawn by an external magnetic field and washed with deionized water and alcohol, and then dried for the next use in the subsequent batch run. As illustrated in Fig. 6d, the DE decreased from 99.0% to 94.9% in five consecutive experimental runs. This implies the appropriate reusability potential of the co-doped sonocatalyst for use in full-scale operation. A drop in the DE of only 4% was observed after the fifth cycle. A similar result was found and reported by Min et al. [60] in the case of a GQD-decorated catalyst [60].

The identification of intermediate by-products of the catalytic oxidation of the target pollutant is of great importance from the environmental aspect. For this purpose, the by-products generated during the sonocatalytic decolorization of MB were determined by GC-MS analysis. The identified by-products of MB degradation were listed in Table 2 . As can be clearly observed, the target pollutant is oxidized to intermediate by-products with lower molecular weight. It is reported that the MB structure can be converted to small aliphatic and aromatic molecules through the cleavage of CC, CC, CN, or CS bonds [52]. Nevertheless, the identification of all intermediate by-products of the sonocatalytic decolorization of MB was not possible due to the limitations of GC-MS and the slight accumulation of by-products. Overall, the conversion of MB to intermediate molecules with lower molecular weight indicated good progress in the mineralization of the target pollutant to low-toxicity or even nontoxic by-products.

NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanoparticles were used for the sonocatalytic decolorization of a textile dye in aqueous media. In this regard, nitrogen and sulfur were incorporated into the GQD lattice, and the product obtained (NS-GQDs) was used to increase the catalytic activity of pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Increasing the sonocatalyst dosage and decreasing the initial solute concentration led to enhancement of decolorization. The treatment process was pH dependent, and the maximum sonocatalytic decolorization was achieved at an initial pH of 8. From the zero-point charge of the sonocatalyst, adsorption played no significant role in the removal of the target organic dye. The doping of magnetite nanoparticles increased the catalytic activity without considerable change in the ferromagnetic properties. The presence of radical-scavenging ions suppressed the decolorization, thereby indirectly indicating the main role of free radicals in degradation of dye molecules in solution. Both persulfate and peroxide increased the efficiency of the NS-GQD/Fe3O4 nanostructures for the generation of free-radical species in the US-based treatment system. In conclusion, NS-GQD/Fe3O4 would be an efficient sonocatalyst for the catalytic decomposition of organic compounds in an aqueous phase with remarkable reusability potential during sequencing cycles, suggesting significant potential for full-scale application in environmental remediation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the University of Tabriz for the support provided.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Data Profile Data Profile

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpcs.2018.12.014.
